coredata.dc:title: Construction of single fluorophore ratiometric pH sensors using dual-emission Mn2+-doped quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Pratiwi, Feby Wijaya
Hsia, Chih-Hao
Kuo, Chiung Wen
Yang, Shun-Min
Hwu, Yeu-Kuang
Chen, Peilin
coredata.dc:description: We present a novel ratiometric pH sensor design using water-soluble, dual-emission, Mn2+-doped quantum dots (Qdots) decorated with D-penicillamine (DPA-MnQdots). In contrast to more commonly used ratiometric pH sensors that rely on the coupling of two fluorophores, our design uses only a single emitter, which simplifies ratiometric sensing and broadens the applications of the sensor. Our single-emitter DPA-MnQdots exhibit two emission bands, at 510nm (green) and 610nm (red), which are, respectively, attributable to exciton recombination and emission of the Mn2+ dopants. The emission intensity ratio (I 510/I 610) of the DPA-MnQdots depends linearly on surrounding pH values within physiological conditions (from pH 4.5 to 8.5). Moreover, the biocompatible DPA-MnQdots were used for long-term monitoring of local pH values in HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: D-penicillamine
Mn2+ quantum dots
Ratiometric sensing
Intracellular pH
Single fluorophore

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and preparation of dual-emission DPA-MnQdots
Ratiometric pH calibration in buffer solution
Internalization and externalization DPA-mn Qdots in to the cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis scheme for MnQdots. (B) Energy diagram for host nanocrystal (purple arrow) and Mn2+ (red arrow) emission. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) PL spectra of MnQdots with different surface modifications at pH 7. (B) PL ratios (I 510/I 610) of MnQdots with different surface modifications at different pH values.

(A) Effect of pH variation on zeta potential. (B) Effect of pH variation on emission intensity. (C) Effects of pH variation on intensity ratio, acquired by fluorometry (repeated 3 times), and individual particles, assessed by microscopy (N=50 particles, repeated 3 times). (D) Reversibility of ratio response intensity as pH is changed from 8 to 5 (the pH was changed 3 separate times). (E) Interference study of DPA-MnQdots at pH 7.

Fluorescence microscopy images acquired at 20, 50, and 80min after delivery into HeLa cells. The histogram shows the numbers of individual particles (N=50) at different pH values. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Mechanism of ratiometric pH sensing using DPA-MnQdots.

head.ce:title: Construction of single fluorophore ratiometric pH sensors using dual-emission Mn2+-doped quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, 1, Section 4, Roosevelt Road, Taipei, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Science and Technology Program, Taiwan International Graduate Program, Academia Sinica and National Taiwan University, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nano Science and Technology Program, Taiwan International Graduate Program, Academia Sinica and National Taiwan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Applied Sciences, Academia Sinica, Taipei, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Applied Sciences, Academia Sinica

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics, Academia Sinica, Taipei, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present a novel ratiometric pH sensor design using water-soluble, dual-emission, Mn2+-doped quantum dots (Qdots) decorated with D-penicillamine (DPA-MnQdots). In contrast to more commonly used ratiometric pH sensors that rely on the coupling of two fluorophores, our design uses only a single emitter, which simplifies ratiometric sensing and broadens the applications of the sensor. Our single-emitter DPA-MnQdots exhibit two emission bands, at 510nm (green) and 610nm (red), which are, respectively, attributable to exciton recombination and emission of the Mn2+ dopants. The emission intensity ratio (I 510/I 610) of the DPA-MnQdots depends linearly on surrounding pH values within physiological conditions (from pH 4.5 to 8.5). Moreover, the biocompatible DPA-MnQdots were used for long-term monitoring of local pH values in HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:para: pH is an essential parameter in the regulation of many cell behaviors, such as protein structures and/or functions, and it also influences crucial cell behaviors including metabolism, proliferation, migration, transformation, and apoptosis (Casey et al., 2010). For example, decreasing intracellular pH can induce apoptosis (Casey et al., 2010; Izumi et al., 2003; Ying and Branchaud, 2011) and the production of acidic organelles (lysosomes and endosomes) during endocytosis and digestion (Ying and Branchaud, 2011). Disruption of intracellular pH causes organelle dysfunction, which can lead to various diseases, including cancer (Kay et al., 2013), Alzheimer's disease (Dennis et al., 2012), and stroke (Uria-Avellanal and Robertson, 2014). Therefore, the ability to monitor the intracellular pH in living cells is very important for studying cellular metabolism and behavior and may facilitate the diagnosis of some diseases.

To investigate the steps involved in drug delivery, including cell uptake, endosomal escape, and cytoplasmic trafficking, it is important to monitor changes in pH microenvironments to improve the rational design and overall understanding of efficient gene delivery (Mintzer and Simanek, 2009) or drugs delivery (Malam et al., 2011). A powerful method for mapping or sensing intracellular pH is fluorescence, a background-free method. Fluorescence-based sensors can remotely, rapidly, and sensitively report intracellular pH status. There are two major categories of fluorescence-based sensors: single-wavelength intensity modulation sensors (Nagaraja et al., 2013; Wen et al., 2013) and ratiometric response-type sensors (Lei et al., 2010; Marín et al., 2012; Peng et al., 2010; Schulz et al., 2010; Shi et al., 2012; Somers et al., 2012). Although single-wavelength intensity modulation methods are very sensitive, there is a risk of artifacts due to potential intensity fluctuations in the light source, detector, optical path length, or probe concentration. To overcome these limitations, ratiometric response sensors have been introduced. In these sensors, the ratio of two emission intensities is used to provide a reliable reading (Liu et al., 2014; Snee et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2003, 2008).

Many ratiometric pH-sensing designs have been reported, including single fluorophores consisting of 2 organic compound units (such as tetraphenylethylene and cyanine (McLaurin et al., 2013), 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole and 1,8-naphthalimide (Soo Choi et al., 2007)) or single fluorophores consisting of 2 functional groups (Cao et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2008). Several different fluorophores have been studied for ratiometric pH sensing. These include the use of two different quantum dots (Qdots) on double sol–gel silica layers (Hiruta et al., 2012); the conjugation of two different carbon dots (Nie et al., 2014) with organic dye (Jing et al., 2015); the use of two different dyes on poly(acrylamide) (Liu et al., 2007), mesoporous silica (Chen et al., 2012; Lei et al., 2010; Tsou et al., 2015), or polymers (Hong et al., 2009; Sun et al., 2011; Wen et al., 2013); and the conjugation of three different fluorophores on polymers (Benjaminsen et al., 2011). Qdots are among the most attractive materials for pH sensing due to their unique optical properties, including high quantum yield, resistance to photobleaching, broad absorption spectra, narrow photoluminescence (PL) spectra, and easily tailored photophysical properties. Generally, Qdot-based ratiometric pH sensors are built by decorating Qdots with additional fluorophores, such as organic dyes (Somers et al., 2012), fluorescent proteins (Wang et al., 2012) or other Qdots with different emission bands (Li et al., 2008). Such designs allow one Qdot to act as a pH-sensitive fluorophore and the other as a pH-inert fluorophore. However, to develop a reliable ratiometric sensor that eliminates false readings due to variations in sensor concentration, two or more fluorophores must be pre-conjugated, which requires the use of sophisticated procedures. Therefore, it is desirable to develop ratiometric sensors using a single fluorophore.

Here, we report the development of a ratiometric pH sensor using a single Qdot that exhibits dual emission signals. In contrast to the more commonly used ratiometric-sensing scheme, which uses coupled fluorophores, only a single type of dual-emission nanoparticle is used in this experiment. The hybrid construct incorporates the photophysical properties of Mn2+-doped Qdots (MnQdots) and the pH-sensitive ligand D-penicillamine (DPA). The DPA-MnQdot emits dual emission bands: under excitation at a single wavelength, green emission occurs at 510nm, and red emission occurs at 610nm. The ratiometric sensing capability is due to the deprotonation and protonation of the carboxylic group on DPA, which caps the surface of the Qdot ( Scheme 1). The intensities of both emissions increase under high-pH conditions. However, the increase in host nanocrystal emission (510nm) is greater than that in Mn2+ emission (610nm). Consequently, the dual emission intensity ratio (I 510/I 610) of the probe gradually increases with increasing pH. This simplifies the ratiometric-sensing design and facilitates corresponding applications, such as imaging. The changes in emission intensity ratios at different pH values allow this probe to be useful for monitoring intracellular pH microenvironments in living cells.

Diphenylphosphine (98%), manganese (II) acetate (98%), sulfur (99.998%), cadmium oxide (CdO, 99.99%), oleylamine (Tech grade), oleic acid (Tech 90%), anhydrous toluene (99.8%), lipoic acid, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) tablets, Hanks‘ Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS), carbodiimide (CDI), D-penicillamine (DPA, 98-101%), cysteine, cysteamine, and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. 1-Octadecene (ODE, 90% Tech) was purchased from Acros Organics. Zinc stearate was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Methoxypolyethylene glycol amine (m-PEG, wt: 2000) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were supplied by Fluka. CellLight Lysosomes-RFP was purchased from Thermo Fisher. Chloroform, ethanol, acetone, n-hexane, and toluene were obtained from JT Baker. Cellu-Sep H1 dialysis membranes were purchased from Membrane Filtration Product Inc. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filters (0.2μm) were purchased from Fisher Scientific.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and preparation of dual-emission DPA-MnQdots
ce:section.ce:para: DPA-MnQdot synthesis was performed using a modified version of a previously published, four-step colloidal synthesis protocol that included the following: (1) synthesis of cores as host particles (CdSSe), (2) Mn2+-dopant growth, (3) host-shell growth, and (4) ligand exchange ( Fig. 1). The details of these steps are provided in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric pH calibration in buffer solution
ce:section.ce:para: A 20-µL aliquot of 150nM DPA-capped Mn2+ CdSSe/ZnS and 180µL aliquots of PBS with different pH values (4.5–8.5) were added to each well of a dark-bottomed 96-well plate. After 5min, PL spectra were recorded based on Ultraviolet–vis absorption (UV–vis) with an excitation wavelength of 405nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a SpectraMax M2 1102 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Molecular Devices).

The calibration was also conducted using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). A 150nM DPA-MnQdot solution was first dispersed in a PBS solution of known pH (in the range of 4.5–8.5), and 1mL of each mixture was dropped onto a coverslip. Images were acquired by CLSM with excitation by a 405-nm laser. The fluorescence intensities of the DPA-MnQdots in the green and red channels were quantified directly from the images using MetaMorph software, and the fluorescence values in the green and red channels were calculated accordingly. An in vitro pH calibration curve was obtained by plotting the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 510nm and 610nm (I 510/I 610) as a function of pH.

Ratiometric pH calibration was performed in HeLa cells. The cells were seeded in a glass-bottomed petri dish at a density of 5×104 and cultured for 24h. The cell culture medium was removed and replaced with PBS containing DPA-MnQdots and then incubated for 2h in 5.0% CO2 at 37°C. To remove excess DPA-MnQdots, the cell samples were washed three times with PBS. After removal of the buffer, the cells were immediately fixed with 1.0% paraformaldehyde for 15min, followed by three rinses with PBS. The PBS was then removed and replaced with PBS buffer with pH values ranging from 4.5 to 8.5 and incubated for 10min. Images were collected using a 60x oil objective with 405-nm excitation. To analyze the images, MetaMorph software was used to calculate the ratio of the fluorescence intensities of the two channels (green and red).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internalization and externalization DPA-mn Qdots in to the cells

ce:section.ce:para: Cells were seeded in a glass-bottomed petri dish at a density of 5×104 and cultured for 24h. The cell culture medium was removed and replaced with PBS containing 150nmol of DPA-MnQdots and then incubated for 10min, 30min, 1h, 2h, 4h and 9h in 5.0% CO2 at 37°C. To remove the excess DPA-MnQdots, the cell samples were washed three times with PBS. After removing the buffer, the cells were digested with trypsin, washed twice with PBS, immediately fixed with 4.0% paraformaldehyde for 15min, and then rinsed twice with PBS. The fixed cells were collected in PBS buffer and analyzed using a flow cytometer with an excitation wavelength of 488nm.

The treatment of cells with DPA-MnQdots was similar to the preparation for flow cytometry. After the removal of the washing buffer, the cells were stained by adding 1µL of cell membrane dye and then incubated for 5min. Then, the cells were washed twice with PBS and immediately fixed with 4.0% paraformaldehyde for 15min, followed by two rinses with PBS. The fixed cells were observed using CLSM.

Dual-emission Qdot was synthesized as illustrated in Fig. 1A, based on the following four-step colloidal synthesis procedure: (1) synthesis of core host particles (CdSSe), (2) Mn2+-dopant growth, (3) host-shell growth, and (4) ligand exchange (Hsia et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2006, 2009). In the first step, CdSSe (a CdS core (bulk CdS band gap≈2.42eV) doped with CdSe (bulk CdSe band gap≈1.74eV)) was used as a core because its excitonic emission can be brought closer to the Mn2+-excited state, allowing efficient coupling (Hsia et al., 2011). ZnS was selected as the shell material because of the availability of well-developed Qdot passivation techniques using water-soluble ligands (Breus et al., 2009; Jing et al., 2015; Susumu et al., 2011). The core used in this experiment was CdSSe with a diameter of 3.1nm. After the doping of Mn2+ and the growth of the ZnS shell, the diameter of the Mn2+-doped CdSSe/ZnS (i.e., the MnQdots) was 8.3nm (Fig. S1A). The MnQdots exhibited a zinc-blended crystal structure, as revealed by distinguishable (111), (220), and (311) planes in its X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S1C). As shown in Fig. 1B, emission from exciton recombination or trapping of the host nanocrystal appears at 510nm (green), while emission from the Mn2+ dopants (4T1→6A1 internal d–d transition) appears at 610nm (red) (Erwin et al., 2005; Nag et al., 2008). The wavelength difference between the dual emission bands reached 100nm and facilitated the accurate calculation of intensity ratios (Aldana et al., 2001; Zhang et al., 2008).

After creating the MnQdots, we investigated the influence of charge on their surfaces at different pH values using four different types of ligands: negatively charged ligands, neutral ligands, positively charged ligands, and zwitterion ligands ( Fig. 2 and Fig. S6). Dihydrolipoic acid (DHLA) and MPA, which are negatively charged ligands, have linear responses to the changing pH. However, these ligands easily precipitated out of solution under acidic conditions with pH values below 6 (Mattoussi et al., 2000; Uyeda et al., 2005) and during the photo-oxidation process (Aldana et al., 2001; Nagaraja et al., 2013). This precipitation occurred because the steric stabilization of these nanoparticles hinges on the deprotonation of the terminal carboxyl groups (Zhan et al., 2013a, 2013b). To observe the effect of combinations of ligands on the sensitivity and stability of the emission, mixtures with different ratios of DHLA (negative) and mPEG-conjugated DHLA (neutral) were used as capping surface ligands. A long-chain ligand such as mPEG can prevent aggregation because it repels other molecules by steric effects; the incoming molecule is not attracted by electrostatic force and cannot penetrate the hydrated PEG layer (Aldana et al., 2001; Liu et al., 2008; Mattoussi et al., 2000; Uyeda et al., 2005). Increasing the amount of mPEG increases stability over a range of pH values but decreases surface charge density, which reduces sensitivity to pH changes. In contrast, the pure positively charged ligand cysteamine (Fig. S6) and neutral ligands mPEG (Fig. 2) on the Qdot surface exhibited less sensitivity to the change of pH. We have also investigated several zwitterion ligands such as a mixture of MPA and cysteamine, cysteine, and DPA. Although both the negative ligands and the zwitterion ligands produced similar trends in pH dependence, we found that only DPA has high stability and sensitivity over a wide pH range. Cysteine and the combination of MPA and cysteamine have low stability in water due to their dimerization process (Liu et al., 2007; Soo Choi et al., 2007). In short, these variations in surface charges influenced the mechanism of the recombination of the exciton hole-pair pathway related to the PL intensity and the stability in the solution.

The presence of ratiometric signal output in the current work fundamentally differs from previous methods using temperature modulation, even the two based on the use of Mn2+-doped Qdots (McLaurin et al., 2013, 2011). “Intra-Qdot modulation” (energy transfer between host excitonic state and Mn2+ 4T1 level) underlies the temperature sensing mechanism of Mn2+-doped Qdots. In contrast, in the current study, ratiometric signal output was also achieved by fluctuating charges on the Qdot surfaces, which were influenced by the changing pH of the solution.

As the pH increased, negatively charged shells gradually appeared on the surfaces of the DPA-MnQdots, as shown in zeta potential values ( Fig. 3A). This process is due to the deprotonation of carboxylic acid groups in DPA (Nagaraja et al., 2013; Uyeda et al., 2005). These negatively charged shells cause changes in trap states and alterations in band gap energy and the electron–hole recombination process. Such a process increases charge carrier confinement in the semiconductor, resulting in higher quantum yields (Maule et al., 2010). Consequently, this pH sensitivity can be been observed by measuring PL lifetime (Orte et al., 2013) and evaluating steady-state PL spectra (Nagaraja et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. S11, the average lifetime (Ƭ) of the DPA-MnQdots depends on pH value. The Ƭ value of the host nanocrystal linearly increases from acid to alkaline condition from 5.57ns±0.26 (s.d.) to 12.21ns±0.25 (s.d.). Similarly, Ƭ value in Mn2+-doped Qdots also increases from 1.075ms ± 0.022 (s.d.) in acid to 1.387 ms± 0.013 (s.d.) in alkaline conditions. Fig. 3B shows that at high pH the emission peaks from both the exciton recombination of the host nanocrystal and the Mn2+ dopants increased (Clapp et al., 2006; Nagaraja et al., 2013; Ruedas-Rama et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2003). However, the increase in the host nanocrystal emission (510nm) is larger than the increase in the Mn2+ emission (610nm), which may be because the host emission is more sensitive to surface changes than the inwardly located Mn2+. Supporting this hypothesis, Fig. S12 shows that the distance of the Mn2+ dopant from the surface affects its pH dependence. Fortunately, there was a linear relationship between the emission ratio and pH value, as shown in Fig. 3C; therefore, DPA-MnQdots can be used as ratiometric pH sensors. In addition, the quantum yield of the DPA-MnQdots was approximately 8–9% (using quinine sulfate as a reference dye).

To prove that DPA-MnQdots are capable of self-referencing (Kawanishi et al., 2000), we examined their emission intensities using these three parameters: varying excitation wavelengths, varying DPA-MnQdot concentrations, and buffer solutions. Fig. S8 (A and B) shows that both of the examined emission intensities (I 510 and I 610) significantly changed as excitation wavelengths and DPA-MnQdots concentrations were varied. However, as shown in Fig. S8 (C and D), changes in the concentration of DPA-MnQdots and excitation wavelength have little impact on the sensing performance due to the ratio of the emission intensity at two different wavelengths (I 510/I 610). This demonstrated that ratiometric probe could be used for accurate detection of the changing pH. Fig. S10 shows that the influence of buffer solutions with differing compositions can also be neglected. In a subsequent experiment, DPA-MnQdots were placed in PBS at 150nM, and the excitation wavelength at 405nm was used.

Because the developed DPA-MnQdot pH sensor is intended to trace local intracellular pH values, it is necessary to evaluate the emission ratios of individual Qdots. We assessed the emission values of individual DPA-MnQdots at different pH values ranging from 4.5 to 8.5 using a CLSM with a 405-nm excitation wavelength. We observed 50 individual particles in one measurement and repeated the measurement 3 times. The pH calibration curves of DPA-MnQdots were measured by fluorometry and microscopy are presented in Fig. 3C. The two sets of experimental data had satisfactory experimental error, verifying the validity of our CLSM quantification method for future live-cell imaging studies.

The key requirements of practical applications using optical sensors include good reversibility and stability. The intensity ratios of DPA-MnQdots were reversible when the buffer solution pH value was changed from 8.0 to 5.0 three separate times (Fig. 3D). The PL intensity ratios were stable over a 12-h measurement (Fig. S7E) and no precipitation was observed after 12h of incubation (Fig. S15B). As shown in Fig. S15A, the hydrodynamic sizes of the Qdots remained the same over a wide range of pH measurements. This stability over a wide range of pH values can be attributed to the structure of the zwitterionic terminal end of DPA and its thiol functional group surrounded by two methyl groups. This configuration poses steric constraints against the oxidative dimerization of the thiol ligands (Breus et al., 2009).

Under physiological conditions, there are many ions and molecules in the solution that may potentially influence the sensing of DPA-MnQdots, such as Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, glucose, and bovine serum albumin (Sivaraman et al., 2014). Therefore, we evaluated the influence of these species on the PL ratio. The results, shown in Fig. 3E, indicate that these compounds have almost no effect on the measurement of pH values at relevant physiological concentrations. We further evaluated the influence of ionic strength by adding different concentrations of NaCl in the buffer solution. As shown in Fig. S9, the I 510/I 610 ratio is not influenced by changes in ionic strength up to 250mM. The neglected effect of ionic strength to the PL ratios was because of the low charge number of DPA-modified quantum dots. Some reports state that highly charged indicators are known to show strong cross-sensitivity to changes in ionic strength, whereas single-charged indicators should be less sensitive (Li et al., 2006; Weidgans et al., 2004; Zhou et al., 2013). These results suggest that these DPA-MnQdots can be used for pH sensing at physiological ionic strength (Tan et al., 2010).

Biocompatibility is one of the most important issues in developing biosensors for living cells. To analyze the biocompatibility of our system, we first examined the cytotoxicity of DPA-MnQdots inside living cells. The results are summarized in Fig. S17. Over 90% of the cells remained viable after treatment with 325nM of DPA-MnQdots for 48h; thus, these Qdots are biocompatible. This result is consistent with previous reports that using DPA as a ligand can reduce Qdot cytotoxicity (Breus et al., 2009; Jiang et al., 2010).

To evaluate the permeability of the DPA-MnQdots, flow cytometry and confocal imaging (Fig. S18) were used to measure the uptake of quantum at different time points (Feng et al., 2014). After 10min of incubation, the DPA-MnQdots began accumulating around the cell surfaces. At each time point, we measured the number of cells with strong fluorescence signal, which represented high quantum dot uptake. After 4h of incubation, more than 58% of the 105 tested cells exhibited strong fluorescence signals, indicating good cell permeability. The fluorescence then gradually decreased at 9h of incubation.

The extracellular environment is slightly alkaline (pH 7.3–7.4), and the DPA ligands on the MnQdots are in a zwitterion form when interacting with the lipid bilayer. The negatively charged Qdots (from the carboxylic group) soften the lipid bilayers, thereby decreasing the risk of creating holes or damaging the membrane during penetration (Kuo et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2012; Zhang and Yang, 2011). Meanwhile, the presence of amino groups with positive charges can facilitate the process of uptake through the plasma membrane, which is negatively charged (Cho et al., 2009; Tan et al., 2010). After 9h, the intensity tended to decrease because the Qdots were gradually secreted from the cells and diluted within the cells due to the increased cell number. Our results demonstrate that surface charge plays an important role in Qdot penetration into cells, internalization, and externalization.

To confirm the validity of our intracellular pH readings, the emission intensity ratios of DPA-MnQdots in live cells at fixed pH values were obtained using CLSM (Fig. S19). A pH vs. I 510/I 610 curve was created, and it was concordant with the pH calibration curve presented in Fig. 3C.

To analyze the DPA-MnQdots in live cells, the DPA-MnQdots were incubated with HeLa cells for 20, 50, and 80min. Following endocytosis, the pH environment experienced by the Qdots changed from alkaline to acidic ( Fig. 4A–C), which could be observed from the color change of the Qdots from green to yellow. The histogram in Fig. 4 shows the numbers of particles at different pH values inside the cells. After a 20-min incubation, the pH values surrounding most of the particles were approximately 7.0–7.5. This result indicates that most of the particles were still in the alkaline culture medium or on the plasma membrane. After 50min of incubation, the number of DPA-MnQdots at pH 6.5 increased, revealing that they had entered the slightly acidic endosomal environment. After an 80-min incubation, the number of DPA-MnQdots at low pH significantly increased, indicating that some of the DPA-MnQdots were in lysosomes, which have pH values as low as 4.5. To further confirm that the DPA-MnQdots were internalized via endocytosis, we also evaluated the co-localization of Qdots with lysosomes using the lysosomal marker CellLight Lysosomes-RFP (Fig. S20). Accordingly, pH changes associated with acute metabolic activity have been suggested to act as signals to regulate functional activities in cells (Dennis et al., 2012; Kay et al., 2013). From this experiment, it is clear that DPA-MnQdots can be used as ratiometric pH sensors in living cells to map pH changes during endocytosis with very low cytotoxicity.

In summary, we successfully developed a new type of ratiometric pH sensor using single Mn2+doped Qdots decorated with DPA. The pH response is attributed to the reversible deprotonation and protonation of carboxylic acid groups on the Qdot surfaces, leading to the change in PL intensity. This ratiometric sensor exhibited the desired structural stability, high selectivity at pH values ranging from 4.5 to 8.5, and high tolerance under physiological ionic strength, which are favorable for cellular pH measurement. As a proof of concept, these DPA-MnQdots have been successfully used to map the pH difference inside the cell during endocytosis. In future work, we will incorporate specific targeting ligands onto MnQdot surfaces to extend our analytic capabilities to specific biological events and functions with potential uses for the early diagnosis of diseases. In fact, the present contribution will provide new insights and methods for the construction of ratiometric sensing and will advance their practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan (contracts MOST 103-2113-M-001-008-MY3) and the Academia Sinica Research Project on Nano Science and Technology (AS-104-TP-A11). CWK acknowledges financial support received from MOST via funding award NSC-102-2113-M-001-019-MY3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.01.079.
